insurance.source: Lloyd's List / HormuzTracker / WEF / Al Jazeera (Apr 28: rates 1-5% hull, 'up to 20x' pre-war)

shipTransit.darkFleetNote: Many vessels transit with AIS disabled. Low AIS count confirms elevated risk — when operators feel safe enough to keep transponders on, that’s the normalcy signal.
shipTransit.source: Windward Maritime Intelligence, Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker (Apr 30: only Iran-linked traffic), Al Jazeera/Kpler, Lloyd's List

oilSpread.brentSource: Yahoo Finance (BZ=F live), CNBC May 1 ($114.01 settle, $126.41 intraday peak Apr 30)
oilSpread.note: Brent June contract hit $126.41 intraday Apr 30 — highest in four years — before settling $114 May 1 after Trump extended ceasefire halting 60-day war powers deadline. Both contracts up ~60% since Feb 28. Dubai benchmark remains 'effectively broken' with widening discount to Brent.

events.event: Formosa Plastics force majeure
events.impact: Major petrochemical supply disruption — ongoing

events.event: Trump deadline for Iran — led to ceasefire Apr 8, then US blockade Apr 12
events.impact: Ceasefire collapsed; US naval blockade now in effect

events.event: Ceasefire agreement — brief strait opening, then collapse
events.impact: Ceasefire lasted ~10 days. Iran briefly reopened strait Apr 17, reclosed Apr 18 after US refused to lift blockade

events.event: US naval blockade of Iranian ports declared
events.impact: US Navy preventing ships from entering/exiting Iranian ports. Freedom of navigation for non-Iranian port traffic

events.event: US SPR 400M barrel release runs dry
events.impact: Emergency buffer exhausted — no more cushion

events.event: US waiver for India buying Russian crude expires
events.impact: India loses alternative supply route

events.event: Iran recloses Strait of Hormuz
events.impact: Iran reversed reopening after US refused to lift blockade. Fired on ships attempting transit. Traffic collapsed to 3/day

events.event: US Navy seizes Iranian-flagged vessel
events.impact: Iran demands immediate release. Ceasefire deadline looming. Tensions at maximum

events.event: Trump extends Iran ceasefire pending negotiations (Pakistan-brokered)
events.impact: Naval blockade maintained. Iran given 3-5 days to engage in talks before resumed strikes. CENTCOM reports 27 vessels intercepted.

events.event: Iran attacks Greek-owned Epaminondas; seizes MSC Francesca
events.impact: Iranian gunboat fired on Epaminondas off Oman with RPGs despite prior clearance. IRGC seized container ship MSC Francesca. US captures M/T Majestic X. 29 vessels intercepted total.

events.event: Trump orders US Navy to destroy Iranian mine-laying boats
events.impact: Standing kill order for any Iranian vessel detected laying mines in the strait. US military estimates 6-month timeline for full mine clearance.

events.event: Iran offers to reopen Strait if US lifts blockade — nuclear talks set aside
events.impact: Tehran's diplomatic opening — decouples strait from nuclear file. Trump confirms discussion with top aides. White House non-committal. ~2,000 ships still stranded in Gulf.

events.event: Brent hits $126/barrel — four-year high
events.impact: June contract spiked to $126.41 intraday on US-Iran escalation fears. Only 1 Iran-linked tanker observed entering Gulf — commercial transit at near-zero.

events.event: Ceasefire extension halts Trump's 60-day war powers deadline
events.impact: Brent retreats to $114 settle. Dual blockade remains in place. Crisis enters Day 63 with no political resolution; refining margins at fresh highs.

bufferMath.source: IEA OECD inventories, EIA Weekly Petroleum Status Report, Goldman Sachs analysis

straitStatus.description: Strait remains effectively closed. Trump extended ceasefire Apr 21 but blockade is unchanged. Iran attacked Greek Epaminondas Apr 22 (RPG fire) and seized MSC Francesca; US Navy has intercepted 29+ vessels including supertankers Dorena, Tifani and Majestic X. Iran offered Apr 28 to reopen strait if US lifts naval blockade — Trump non-committal. Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker (Apr 30): only 1 Iran-linked tanker entered Gulf, no commercial exits. Estimated 2,000 ships still stranded. US military says full mine clearance will take ~6 months.

alternativeRoutes.description: Now the dominant rerouting path — capacity bottlenecks at East African ports worsening. War-risk surcharge component rising as VLCC rates climb.
Pipeline saturated; cannot compensate for maritime closure. Saudi drone strike on key pumping station temporarily disrupted throughput in mid-April.
Constrained by ongoing Red Sea security risk and Houthi threats; insurance costs rising in tandem with Gulf premiums.

regionalImpact.description: Imports 40% of oil and 30% of LNG via Hormuz. 55 Chinese-flagged ships trapped in Persian Gulf.
Heavily dependent on Middle East oil via Hormuz; supply disruption threatens industrial production.
Import-dependent nation; oil import costs surging, affecting refiner margins and energy sector.
12-14% of LNG sourced from Qatar via Hormuz; alternative sourcing expensive and limited.
Permitted limited vessel transits through negotiation; still facing supply constraints and higher costs.

crisisTimeline.title: Brent settles $114 after $126 peak; ceasefire halts 60-day war powers deadline
crisisTimeline.description: Brent retreats to $114.01 settle after intraday spike to $126.41 on Apr 30 (June contract, four-year high). White House confirms ceasefire extension halts Trump's 60-day war powers clock. Crack spreads at fresh highs — gasoline crack at $47/bbl, heating oil at $66/bbl. Dual blockade unchanged.

crisisTimeline.title: Brent intraday $126.41 — highest in four years
crisisTimeline.description: Brent June contract spiked to $126.41 on US-Iran escalation fears before retreating. Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker: only one Iran-linked fuel tanker observed entering Persian Gulf for the day. Commercial transits at near-zero — both Brent and WTI up ~60% since Feb 28.

crisisTimeline.title: Iran offers to reopen Strait if US lifts blockade — nuclear talks set aside
crisisTimeline.description: Tehran's foreign ministry decouples Hormuz from nuclear file. Trump discusses proposal with Rubio and top aides; White House non-committal. ~2,000 ships still stranded in Gulf. Underwriters say transit confidence won't return until 'sustained normal vessel movement, not isolated transits.'

crisisTimeline.title: US intercepts M/V Sevan; Russian superyacht Nord transits unchallenged
crisisTimeline.description: USS-led interception of Panama-flagged propane/butane carrier M/V Sevan in Arabian Sea on suspected Iran-linked cargo. Russian superyacht Nord crossed the Gulf without incident from either navy — selective enforcement signal.

crisisTimeline.title: USS Rafael Peralta captures unidentified Iranian oil tanker
crisisTimeline.description: US Navy seizure inside Persian Gulf — first US destroyer-led capture of an Iranian-flagged tanker within Gulf waters since blockade began Apr 13.

crisisTimeline.title: Trump orders US Navy to destroy Iranian mine-laying boats
crisisTimeline.description: Standing kill order for any Iranian vessel laying mines in strait. US military says full mine clearance — once strait reopens — will take approximately six months. Underwriters cite mine-clearance timeline as gating factor for premium normalization.

crisisTimeline.title: Iran attacks Greek Epaminondas off Oman; seizes MSC Francesca
crisisTimeline.description: Iranian gunboat fired RPGs at Greek-owned Epaminondas despite prior transit clearance, causing significant bridge damage. IRGC also seized Liberia-flagged container ship MSC Francesca. US captured VLCC Majestic X. CENTCOM reports 29 vessels intercepted total. Trump claims blockade costs Iran $500M/day.

crisisTimeline.title: Trump extends ceasefire pending negotiations — Pakistan brokers
crisisTimeline.description: Trump declared ceasefire extension at Pakistan's request to allow Iranian proposal. Naval blockade maintained. US officials gave Iran 3-5 days to engage in talks before resuming strikes. CENTCOM intercepts at 27 vessels. US captures supertanker Tifani in Indo-Pacific.

crisisTimeline.title: US seizes Iranian-flagged vessel; Iran demands release
crisisTimeline.description: US Navy fired on and seized an Iranian cargo ship defying the blockade. Iran demands immediate release as ceasefire deadline looms. Tensions at maximum — traffic through strait at near-standstill (3 vessels/day).

crisisTimeline.title: Iran recloses Strait — fires on ships, 3 vessels attacked
crisisTimeline.description: Iran reversed its reopening after US refused to lift naval blockade. Iranian forces fired on Indian-flagged ships and CMA CGM Everglade (rocket damage). 35 outbound vessels reversed course. 6 stranded cruise ships evacuated through brief window.

crisisTimeline.title: Iran announces strait open during Lebanon truce — short-lived
crisisTimeline.description: Iran declared commercial passage 'completely open' during Lebanon ceasefire. Trump clarified US blockade on Iranian ports remains. Iran cancelled reopening within 24 hours.

crisisTimeline.title: US declares naval blockade of Iranian ports
crisisTimeline.description: After Islamabad talks failed, Trump declared naval blockade preventing ships from entering/exiting Iranian ports. Freedom of navigation maintained for non-Iranian port traffic. Transit dropped from 17/day to 6/day.

crisisTimeline.title: US Navy destroyers enter strait for mine clearance
crisisTimeline.description: First US Navy entry into the strait since hostilities began. Mine clearance operations commence. Transit surges briefly to 17 vessels as ships attempt to exploit ceasefire window.

crisisTimeline.title: Ceasefire agreement reached — Iran charges $1M+ tolls
crisisTimeline.description: Temporary ceasefire agreed. Iran began controlling traffic and charging tolls exceeding $1M per ship. 45 ships transited in first 4 days. Gulf states intercepted missiles hours into ceasefire.

crisisTimeline.title: Iranian attacks on Gulf states surge to monthly high
crisisTimeline.description: Bloomberg Intelligence data shows Iranian attacks spiking. UAE intercepted 537 ballistic missiles, 2,256 drones, 26 cruise missiles cumulatively. Iran's 'Mosaic Strategy' continues.

crisisTimeline.title: Status quo: controlled transit, ongoing negotiations
crisisTimeline.description: Strait under selective Iranian control with ~6% of normal traffic. Trump extended deadline to April 6. Estimated 2000 ships stranded. War risk insurance still 6-7x normal.

crisisTimeline.title: China-Pakistan present 5-point ceasefire initiative
crisisTimeline.description: China and Pakistan jointly present peace proposal including immediate ceasefire, halt to infrastructure attacks, safe passage through Strait, and framework for comprehensive peace.

crisisTimeline.title: Iran selectively allows approved nations to transit
crisisTimeline.description: Iran permits passage for vessels from China, Russia, India, Iraq, Pakistan. Malaysia and Thailand granted access following direct talks. UN humanitarian and fertilizer shipments permitted.

crisisTimeline.title: Trump issues 48-hour ultimatum to open Strait
crisisTimeline.description: Trump warns Iran that power plants will be obliterated unless Strait fully opens within 48 hours. Iran responds with threats to target US/Israeli energy infrastructure and lay sea mines.

crisisTimeline.title: US releases 400M barrels from Strategic Petroleum Reserve
crisisTimeline.description: Largest SPR release in history. Estimated 95 days of supply at current drawdown rate. Global coordinated release with IEA members.

crisisTimeline.title: US Navy deploys carrier strike group to Gulf of Oman
crisisTimeline.description: USS Eisenhower carrier strike group positioned in Gulf of Oman. Additional destroyer deployments to escort allied vessels. Iran IRGC navy conducts counter-exercises.

crisisTimeline.title: Iran restricts Strait of Hormuz transit
crisisTimeline.description: Iran begins controlled transit regime following escalation of US-Iran tensions over nuclear program. Initial blockade affects all non-allied vessels. Oil prices spike 15% overnight.

phases.description: Diplomatic resolution, ceasefire agreement, and physical reopening of strait. China-Pakistan 5-point ceasefire initiative in progress.

Even after strait reopens, it takes 2-3 months for tanker schedules, port logistics, and crude flows to renormalize. Ships need to reposition, insurance rates need to fall, and loading schedules must reset.
Steam crackers and shut-in refineries across Asia need full restart cycles. Naphtha supply chain must rebuild. Force majeures across petrochemical sector will take months to unwind.
Inventory rebuilding, SPR replenishment, insurance rate normalization, and return to pre-crisis pricing. Consensus estimate: September 2026 at the earliest.

recoveryClock.keyInsight: Asia passed the point of no return by the second week of the closure. Even if the strait reopens tomorrow, 4-5 months before any sense of normalcy returns. Add 6 months for full mine clearance per US Navy estimate (Apr 23) — that pushes baseline normalization into Q1 2027. — Jun Goh, Sparta Commodities
recoveryClock.source: Sparta Commodities / MB Commodity Corner / Palmer Energy / US Navy mine-clearance estimate (Apr 23)

countries.notes: SPR release exhausted as of ~Apr 15. 400M barrels depleted. No more emergency buffer. Remaining 300M barrels in reserve are not authorized for release.
IEA coordinated release. 65% oil import dependency via Hormuz. Industrial production threatened.
IEA member. 70% Hormuz dependency. Refiner margins under severe pressure.
Has NOT tapped strategic reserves despite being largest importer. Massive stockpiles untouched. Reduced run rates + product export ban instead.
Limited SPR capacity. 45% Hormuz dependency. Permitted limited transits via negotiation.

sprStatus.chinaSignal: China holds ~950M-1.3bn barrels in strategic reserves (largest in the world) and has still NOT released any as of May 1. Japan and South Korea (IEA members) have already released. China's continued forbearance — even with Brent at $126 intraday Apr 30 — is the single most important crisis-tier signal we track. When China taps its SPR, it signals peak crisis.

events.event: 50-liter petrol rationing for 4-wheel vehicles
events.impact: President announced fuel cap to prevent panic during Eid al-Fitr exodus period

events.event: Product export ban + reduced refinery run rates
events.impact: State + teapot refineries cut runs; only producing for domestic. Australia, Philippines, Vietnam losing Chinese fuel supply

events.event: Selling diesel to Philippines & Vietnam
events.impact: Selective diesel exports to neighbors — shows China managing regional supply as strategic lever

events.event: Maximizing coal power capacity factors
events.impact: Switching from gas to coal for power generation to reduce oil/gas dependency

events.event: Petrochemical force majeures across region
events.impact: Chandra Asri, IPCC, and multiple SE Asian petrochemical players declare force majeure. Naphtha imports stopped.

events.event: Fuel supply disruption from China export cuts
events.impact: Major importer of Chinese refined products. Scrambling for alternative diesel and fuel supplies.

transmissionChain: Emerging markets suffer — Asia, Europe still absorbing
Global recession if this doesn't go back
inflationThreshold.notes: Brent rebounded from $91 (Apr 20) to a $126 intraday peak Apr 30 — four-year high — after Iran's Epaminondas attack and MSC Francesca seizure killed the ceasefire-driven sell-off. Settled $114 May 1 after Trump extended the ceasefire (halting his 60-day war powers deadline). EIA forecasts US retail gasoline above $4.30/gal and diesel above $5.80/gal — record seasonal highs. With Brent sustained above $105 for most of the crisis, March CPI is set to print 3.2-3.5%, leaving the Fed boxed.

tankerRates.source: Fearnleys (fearnpulse.com), S&P Global, Maritime Hub, Lloyd's List
tankerRates.context: VLCC spot rates re-accelerated through late April as the dual blockade held and Iran attacked the Epaminondas (Apr 22) and seized MSC Francesca. Persian Gulf VLCC loadings (60% of 2025 loadings) remain near-zero. Rates spiked further on the Apr 30 Brent surge to $126. Many VLCCs continue to ballast to Atlantic basin loadings. Europe-Gulf container rotation still on Cape route (41 vs 25 days), adding $300-400/TEU. War-risk premium component now ~30% above pre-crisis equivalent rates.

months.note: Final April count — highest monthly total of crisis. UAE single-day record on Apr 22 (Epaminondas attack day). Cumulative UAE intercepts: 537 ballistic missiles, 2,256 drones, 26 cruise missiles
Through May 1 — ceasefire extension Apr 21 reduced strike tempo but Mosaic Strategy attacks continue

iranianAttacks.context: Iranian attacks on Gulf states peaked in April 2026 at 232 incidents — highest monthly total of the crisis. Strikes spiked again Apr 22 after Iran attacked the Greek Epaminondas and seized MSC Francesca. Trump's ceasefire extension Apr 21 reduced overall tempo into May but did not stop decentralized Mosaic Strategy attacks.
iranianAttacks.mosaicStrategyNote: Iran's 'Mosaic Strategy' — sector commanders have autonomy to launch missiles and drones without central approval. The uptick is being dismissed as decentralized action, not coordinated escalation.
headlines: Brent intraday $126 — highest in four years (Apr 30)
Iran offers to reopen Strait if US lifts blockade — nuclear talks set aside (Apr 28)
Iran attacks Greek Epaminondas with RPGs; seizes MSC Francesca (Apr 22)
Trump orders Navy to destroy Iranian mine-laying boats (Apr 23)
US captures supertankers Dorena, Tifani, Majestic X — 29 vessels intercepted
Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker: only Iran-linked traffic moves (Apr 30)
iranianAttacks.source: Bloomberg Intelligence, Windward Maritime Intelligence, Critical Threats, NPR, Al Jazeera

refiningMargins.context: Crack spreads pushed to fresh crisis highs through the Apr 21 ceasefire extension. Gasoline crack peaked at $48.5/bbl on Apr 30 (vs $41 on Apr 21, prev cycle peak $34.2 in March), heating oil at $67.5 (vs $60 on Apr 21, prev peak $58.3). 3-2-1 crack at ~$47/bbl per RBN. Diesel/jet refining margins at highest levels since early 2024. Margins continue accelerating despite Brent retreating off the $126 Apr 30 peak — refined product scarcity is now the dominant constraint. Still NOT a topping signal — margins must reverse before the bearish thesis activates.
refiningMargins.hfiThesis: If you want to know when it's time to get bearish oil, just watch refining margins. In COVID, floating storage, tanker rates, contango, and refining margins all peaked first. Then margins went negative. When they rebounded, crude rallied. This time around, a similar playbook will happen but in reverse — refining margins will be the key indicator for when the global economy cannot handle higher prices any longer.
sellTriggers: Crack spreads sustain a move back toward pre-crisis baseline (~$15 gas, ~$25 HO)
Margins go negative — refineries literally losing money on every barrel
Backwardation compresses to flat or contango
SE Asian refineries start cutting throughput rates
refiningMargins.source: HFI Research (@HFI_Research), Hemisphere Research Capital (@hemis_research), RBN Energy 3-2-1, EIA
